title: Who’s at the Door?
subtitle: Foundations: fraud + money laundering = FRAML

hook.metaphor: Picture the bouncer outside a busy club. Two very different problems walk up. One is the person with a *fake ID* trying to sneak in — that's **fraud**: deception to get something they shouldn't. The other looks respectable but is quietly trying to *wash a bag of dirty cash* through the cloakroom — that's **money laundering**. A good bouncer watches for both, which is exactly why modern teams combine them into one job: **FRAML**.
hook.scenario: A payments firm has two teams that never talk: a fraud team and an anti-money-laundering team. A criminal uses a stolen card (fraud) and then funnels the proceeds through mule accounts (laundering) — and slips through the gap *between* the two teams. What was the real weakness?

learn: **Financial crime** is an umbrella for illegal ways of moving or taking money. The two giants under it are **fraud** and **money laundering** — and although they're different, they're deeply connected: fraud *generates* dirty money, and laundering *hides* where it came from. They're two ends of the same criminal pipeline.
**Fraud is deception for gain** — tricking a person or system into handing over money or access. The victim can be the firm, the customer, or both.

**Money laundering is disguise** — taking the proceeds of crime ('dirty' money) and making them look legitimate ('clean'), so criminals can spend the profits without getting caught. No victim hands over money in the moment; the harm is to the whole financial system's integrity.

Because criminals don't respect org charts, firms increasingly merge the two disciplines into **FRAML (Fraud + AML).** Our scenario's criminal exploited the *gap* between siloed teams — a unified view of the customer would have connected the stolen card to the mule accounts. The scale is staggering: financial crime drains trillions globally each year, which is why regulators worldwide — Australia's **AUSTRAC**, NZ's FIU, India's **FIU-IND**, and the global standard-setter **FATF** — police it hard, with heavy penalties for firms that don't.
bridge: So at work, you can hold two questions in your head about any suspicious activity: *Is someone being deceived to give up money or access?* (fraud) and *Is someone trying to disguise where money came from?* (laundering). The smartest financial-crime defences refuse to treat these as separate problems — because criminals never do.

interactions.title: Mind the gap
interactions.prompt: The criminal slipped through the gap between the siloed fraud and AML teams. What's the core lesson?

options.label: Hire more fraud analysts.
options.feedback: More hands might help, but it misses the point: the failure was the *gap between* the teams, not a shortage in one of them.

options.label: Unite fraud and AML into one connected view (FRAML).
options.feedback: Exactly. The stolen card (fraud) and the mule accounts (laundering) were one criminal story. Connecting the signals — FRAML — closes the gap criminals exploit.

options.label: Accept that some criminals always get through.
options.feedback: Some leakage is realistic, but this loss was preventable. Shrugging at a structural blind spot is exactly what regulators penalise.

interactions.title: Fraud or laundering?
interactions.prompt: Sort each act. Remember: fraud = deception for gain; laundering = disguising dirty money.

items.label: Using a stolen credit card to buy goods
Funnelling crime proceeds through fake businesses
Tricking someone into paying a fake invoice
Buying property to make dirty cash look legitimate

interactions.title: Financial-crime basics
interactions.prompt: Match each term to its meaning.

pairs.right: Deceiving a person or system to gain money or access
Making the proceeds of crime look legitimate
Running fraud and anti-money-laundering defences together
The global body that sets anti-financial-crime standards
Australia's financial-crime regulator and intelligence unit

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “Financial crime is mostly fraud (deceiving someone for money) and money laundering (disguising dirty money) — and since they're two ends of one pipeline, smart firms fight them together as FRAML.”

checkpoint.q: The key difference between fraud and money laundering is…
options: Fraud is legal; laundering is not
Fraud is deception for gain; laundering disguises the proceeds of crime
They are exactly the same thing
checkpoint.explain: Fraud tricks someone out of money/access; laundering hides where already-dirty money came from.

checkpoint.q: Why combine fraud and AML teams into 'FRAML'?
options: Because criminals exploit the gaps between siloed teams
Because the law forbids separate teams
checkpoint.explain: Fraud generates dirty money and laundering hides it — one pipeline; a connected view closes the gap.

options: The global standard-setter for fighting financial crime
checkpoint.explain: The Financial Action Task Force sets the international AML/CFT standards that national regulators implement.
